RT @JessePeltan: Remember when the problem with EVs was the cost of the battery?

Yeah. Not a problem anymore. https://t.co/yC1RkiUPmz

retweet.text:
Remember when the problem with EVs was the cost of the battery?

Yeah. Not a problem anymore. https://t.co/yC1RkiUPmz

quoted.text:
✍️ New article: Battery costs have declined by 99% in the last three decades, making electrified transport a reality—

Over 20 million electric cars were sold globally in 2025 — some for as little as $10,000. Even just two decades ago, that would have been impossible.

The reason it's possible now? Batteries have gotten *much* cheaper.

In 1991, lithium-ion battery cells cost around $9,200 per kilowatt-hour. By 2024, that had fallen to just $78 — a decline of more than 99%. You can see this in the chart.

To put that in perspective: the battery cells in a standard electric car today cost around $5,000. In 1991, those same cells would have cost nearly $600,000.

There was no single breakthrough behind this. Batteries follow a “learning curve”: as cumulative production grows, thousands of small improvements in chemistry, manufacturing, and supply chains drive prices down.

Since 1998, every time global cumulative battery production doubled, the price dropped by roughly 19%.

Early progress was driven by consumer electronics — phones and laptops — before the technology became viable for cars, buses, and larger energy storage.

Energy density has also more than tripled since the 1990s, meaning batteries can now store far more energy for their volume.

The half-a-million-dollar battery was never going to transform transport. The $5,000 battery is.

tweets.text:
RT @BritHugoboom: Grokipedia is far superior to Wikipedia and it’s not even close, especially when it comes to neutrality.

Take Evie’s Wik…

retweet.text:
Grokipedia is far superior to Wikipedia and it’s not even close, especially when it comes to neutrality.

Take Evie’s Wikipedia page:  it’s been the target of a years-long hit job by a group of leftist activist editors who are blatantly breaking Wiki’s own rules, and they do this by defending each other’s edits while claiming consensus. When a factual edit is made, it’s immediately reversed because they’re watching the page. 

Grokipedia actually stays neutral and summarizes facts. It pulls straight from our own website and social media pages to describe what kind of content we publish (shocking!), instead of letting some opinionated leftist journalist slap their spin on everything. 

It reviews all sources, weighs bias, and references articles from the larger, widely trusted publications, instead of using Media Matters or Vice as “neutral sources” (lol). 

This is such important work from xAI because all these LLMs are out there scraping Wikipedia for their answers. If Wikipedia is full of false info or pushing a heavily biased take, then every AI chatbot ends up repeating the same garbage.

Thank you @elonmusk.

user.name: Brittany Hugoboom
user.description: Founder @evie_magazine • Founder @28wellness 💕 “The Glamorous Editor of Evie” - WSJ • “Seems, to me, a bit more like the provocateur Camille Paglia.” - VF
